evidence: goseg/protocol/contracts/governance/manifest.go defines upload actions, c2c actions, and startram error contracts in one shared manifest (constants and declaration slices), creating one hub module for unrelated domains.

goseg/uploadsvc/doc.go names goseg/protocol/contracts/governance/manifest.go as the canonical declaration location, coupling uploadsvc documentation and policy understanding to a specific cross-domain file rather than a stable package-level boundary.

goseg/protocol/contracts/conformance/fixtures_action_test.go and goseg/uploadsvc/actions_test.go both assert action-contract behavior from different package viewpoints, indicating cross-package contract verification logic is distributed rather than flowing through a single boundary.

Process-wide mutable default runtimes are used as implicit dependencies: broadcast keeps `defaultBroadcastStateRuntime` and `defaultBroadcastLoopController` globals with setters/resets, while docker events keeps `defaultEventRuntime` with `SetDefaultEventRuntime`/`ResetDefaultEventRuntime`.

Core workflows bind to those mutable defaults instead of explicit runtime ownership (for example `shipworkflow.newWorkflowRuntime` and `importer.defaultImporterRuntime` use `events.NewGlobalDefaultBroker`).

Runtime abstraction is dominated by wide callback bags: `startuporchestrator` splits startup into multiple embedded runtime structs with many callback fields, `importerRuntime` embeds three runtime groups, and `docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go` defines large `Runtime*Ops` families and seam bundle builders.

Tests reflect high indirection cost: startup/runtime tests construct extensive override helpers (`withStartBackgroundServiceCallback`, `testWireguardRuntime`, runtime option layers) primarily to satisfy callback-bag shapes rather than focused domain contracts.

goseg/uploadsvc/actions_test.go has good table coverage for ValidateCommand and ParseUploadAction, but all assertions are internal to uploadsvc and do not exercise governance declarations as test inputs.

goseg/protocol/contracts/conformance/fixtures_action_test.go checks fixture completeness/matching for canonical bindings, but does not include an integration-style assertion that uploadsvc accepted/rejected payloads are consistent with RequiredPayloads/ForbiddenPayloads from goseg/protocol/contracts/governance/manifest.go.

README.md defines backend quality gates including contract conformance for upload payload rules, but the provided test files show no explicit end-to-end test binding that gate to runtime behavior across protocol/contracts and uploadsvc.

Global runtime singleton management is interleaved with domain behavior in multiple modules (e.g., default runtime vars plus Default/Set/Reset APIs in goseg/broadcast/state.go and goseg/docker/events/events.go), so each file mixes business operations with process-wide lifecycle control.

Configuration patch handling centralizes unrelated responsibilities in one schema file: patch shape definitions, generic parser framework, parse/apply/merge registry construction, and concrete parsers all live in goseg/config/config_update_schema.go while duplicating config surface definitions maintained in goseg/structs/configs.go.

wifiRuntime.toggleDevice in goseg/system/wifi_runtime_methods.go combines device resolution, partial-result degradation policy, radio-state decision logic, command construction, and command execution; test coverage in goseg/system/wifi_test.go reflects this broad orchestration burden with many branch-heavy cases.

Large test files aggregate many independent behaviors and fixtures in single files (e.g., uploader/session/header/chunk/finalization coverage mixed in goseg/importer/importer_test.go, and filesystem/mount/fstab/path lifecycle coverage mixed in goseg/system/storage/storage_test.go), increasing change blast radius.
goseg/broadcast/loop.go:212 drops the result of AddSystemTransitionError via `_ = ...`, while the callee can return ErrBroadcastRuntimeRequired (goseg/broadcast/state.go:71-76).

goseg/docker/events/events.go:147-150 maps all context cancellation/deadline exits to ErrTransitionPublishTimeout instead of preserving ctx.Err(), unlike many other modules that wrap underlying causes with `%w` (for example goseg/importer/importer.go:561-616).

goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go:574-575 wires routinesystem.RunChopRoutinesWithContext and RunBackupRoutinesWithContext as monitored background services, but goseg/routines/system/system.go:40-49 and 102-109 always return nil after spawning internal goroutines, preventing startup health supervision from receiving worker failures.

findings.summary: A single governance manifest acts as a cross-domain hub for unrelated contract families, increasing change blast radius.
evidence: manifest.go mixes Upload, C2C, and Startram contracts in one package-level declaration surface (`actionDeclarations`, `startramContractDeclarations`, and multiple domain constants).
uploadsvc/doc.go points directly to `contracts/governance/manifest.go` as canonical, reinforcing dependency on a broad internal hub instead of a narrower domain contract facade.
findings.suggestion: Split governance declarations by domain/family (for example upload, c2c, startram) behind a small composition API in governance; keep only shared schema primitives in the root manifest package so domain modules depend on narrower boundaries.

findings.summary: Contract conformance checks are spread across package-local and cross-package tests, which weakens a single authoritative verification boundary.
evidence: fixtures_action_test.go validates canonical action bindings and fixture completeness at protocol level.
actions_test.go separately encodes upload action acceptance and payload-policy expectations via local test tables.
doc.go describes canonical conformance and governance locations, but tests remain split across layers, so adding/changing actions requires synchronized updates in multiple modules.
findings.suggestion: Define one contract-test entrypoint (shared helper or generated cases from governance declarations) that module tests consume, so package-level tests verify behavior while contract shape/rules are sourced from one authoritative boundary.

findings.summary: Mutable process-default runtimes create leaky, hidden dependencies across subsystems.
evidence: `events` exposes mutable global runtime state (`defaultEventRuntime`) and a broker (`NewGlobalDefaultBroker`) that resolves against that mutable default on every call.
`broadcast` similarly stores mutable process defaults (`defaultBroadcastStateRuntime`, `defaultBroadcastLoopController`) with setter/reset APIs used by runtime constructors.
Workflow/importer defaults are wired to the global broker (`events.NewGlobalDefaultBroker`), so behavior can be changed out-of-band by global runtime mutation.
findings.suggestion: Replace mutable process-default brokers with explicit runtime ownership per subsystem lifecycle (startup creates one broker/runtime instance and passes it down). Keep global defaults only as legacy compatibility shims behind a narrow boundary, then migrate call sites (`shipworkflow`, `importer`, `broadcast`) to constructor-injected brokers.

findings.summary: Large callback-bag runtime structs add indirection with weak domain leverage.
evidence: `startuporchestrator` composes startup runtime from multiple embedded callback structs and maps service-name enums to callback fields, creating orchestration-by-field-name instead of stable service interfaces.
`importerRuntime` embeds storage/upload/provision callback groups with many function fields and repeated validation groups, indicating runtime shape is acting as a generic dependency bag.
`runtime_ops.go` defines many `Runtime*Ops` structs and seam builders that mostly forward package-level functions into function fields.
Tests need extensive option builders and callback overrides to exercise behavior, which indicates high abstraction ceremony and low leverage from these runtime surfaces.
findings.suggestion: Refactor callback bags into smaller behavior-oriented interfaces owned by use-case boundaries (for example `TransitionPublisher`, `ContainerProvisioner`, `StartupServiceRunner`). Keep only a minimal composition root that adapts concrete packages to those interfaces; remove field-by-field callback maps and service-name switch dispatch.

findings.summary: Several runtime wrapper layers are pass-through and increase call depth without new policy.
evidence: Broadcast lifecycle exposes paired wrappers (`LoadStartramRegionsWithRuntime` -> `LoadStartramRegionsWithRuntimeState`, `bootstrapBroadcastState` -> `bootstrapBroadcastStateWithRuntime`) that mainly re-check nil and forward.
Broadcast loop frequently reconstructs default runtime wrappers (`newBroadcastLoopRuntimeWithDefault`) for operations that immediately delegate.
WiFi runtime service repeatedly performs prepare/resolve forwarding before invoking runtime methods, while runtime methods already wrap command-level operations and defaults.
findings.suggestion: Collapse pass-through wrapper pairs into one canonical entry point per operation, and centralize nil/default resolution once at construction. Keep wrappers only when they enforce distinct policy (auth, retries, timeout semantics); otherwise remove redundant forwarding layers.

findings.summary: Canonical contract governance and upload runtime validation are documented as linked but enforced in separate modules without a direct executable boundary contract.
evidence: uploadsvc/doc.go states canonical declarations live in governance manifest.
actions_test.go validates ParseUploadAction/ValidateCommand only against uploadsvc-local constants and errors.
fixtures_action_test.go validates protocol/contracts fixtures and bindings, not uploadsvc runtime behavior against governance metadata.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a shared contract-validation adapter (or integration test harness) that consumes governance ActionDeclarations and asserts uploadsvc parser/validator behavior matches namespace/action and payload rules, so contract ownership is executable across module boundaries.

findings.summary: Quality-gate intent is documented, but tests do not include a cross-module check that governance payload policy and upload runtime validation stay in lockstep.
evidence: README backend quality gate calls out contract conformance and upload payload-rule checks.
governance/manifest.go defines RequiredPayloads/ForbiddenPayloads policy metadata.
actions_test.go checks upload validator outcomes without deriving expectations from governance metadata.
fixtures_action_test.go verifies canonical fixture/binding set integrity but not runtime enforcement in uploadsvc.
findings.suggestion: Add integration tests that iterate governance UploadActionDeclarations and assert uploadsvc ValidateCommand rejects forbidden payload combinations and accepts required ones, making the quality gate a direct compatibility check.

findings.summary: Runtime domain APIs also own process-global singleton lifecycle, blurring module responsibility boundaries.
evidence: goseg/broadcast/state.go defines state mutation/query behavior and also process-global runtime storage + mutators (`defaultBroadcastStateRuntime`, `DefaultBroadcastStateRuntime`, `SetDefaultBroadcastStateRuntime`, `ResetDefaultBroadcastStateRuntime`).

goseg/docker/events/events.go follows the same pattern with event publish/subscribe behavior plus mutable global default runtime (`defaultEventRuntime`, `DefaultEventRuntime`, `SetDefaultEventRuntime`, `ResetDefaultEventRuntime`).
This repeats a structural pattern where runtime logic and app-global lifecycle policy are co-located, making the same files change for unrelated reasons.
findings.suggestion: Extract singleton lifecycle ownership into dedicated runtime-provider files (e.g., `*_runtime_default.go`) and keep runtime types/files focused on pure event/state operations. Keep `Default/Set/Reset` wrappers thin and isolated so domain modules no longer carry global lifecycle policy.

findings.summary: Config patch file mixes schema definition, parsing engine, and merge/apply orchestration in one unit.
evidence: goseg/config/config_update_schema.go contains patch DTOs, registry model (`configPatchField`), generic parsing helpers, concrete parse functions, and registry construction/validation in a single file.

Patch sections mirror many fields from persistent config types in goseg/structs/configs.go (connectivity/runtime/startram/penpai/auth-session), creating parallel schema surfaces maintained across files.
The mixed engine+schema placement raises coordination cost because model changes and parsing framework changes are coupled in one hotspot.
findings.suggestion: Split into section-scoped units: keep patch DTO/schema definitions near config models, move parse/apply registry mechanics into a separate engine package/file, and define field mappings per section so adding/removing config fields does not require touching one monolithic orchestrator.

findings.summary: WiFi toggle path combines fallback policy, state inference, and command execution in one method.
evidence: In goseg/system/wifi_runtime_methods.go, `toggleDevice` handles empty-input device resolution (`primaryWifiDevice`), partial-result handling (`ErrWiFiPartialResult` warning path), radio-state check (`isWiFiRadioEnabled`), target command derivation (`up/down`), and shell invocation.

The same file mixes command and policy layers, while goseg/system/wifi_test.go carries many targeted branches for these combinations (device parsing, strict vs best-effort behavior, toggle seam behavior), indicating one method is covering multiple concerns.
findings.suggestion: Refactor `toggleDevice` into small steps with explicit seams: `resolveToggleTarget`, `deriveToggleCommand`, and `executeLinkToggle`. Keep partial-result policy in one helper reused by other WiFi flows to reduce duplicated branching semantics.

findings.summary: Several large tests bundle unrelated behavior groups, reducing test module coherence.
evidence: goseg/importer/importer_test.go (~1000 LOC) combines endpoint authorization, HTTP header validation, session lifecycle, chunk persistence/combination, post-import workflow, and finalize behavior in one file.
goseg/system/storage/storage_test.go (~650 LOC) combines SMART state handling, mount detection, tmp-dir reconciliation, fstab parsing/reconciliation, filesystem formatting, and create-path behavior.
These files contain many independent domains and helper fixtures, so a small domain change causes wide, noisy diffs in the same test modules.
findings.suggestion: Split each into behavior-focused suites (e.g., `importer_endpoint_test.go`, `importer_chunks_test.go`, `importer_finalize_test.go`; `storage_mount_test.go`, `storage_fstab_test.go`, `storage_filesystem_test.go`) and move common fixtures into narrow shared helpers per sub-domain.

findings.summary: Broadcast tick error path suppresses secondary persistence failure, causing inconsistent failure visibility.
evidence: goseg/broadcast/loop.go:210-213 handles buildBroadcastTickState failure, then calls `_ = stateRuntime.AddSystemTransitionError(err.Error())` and returns only the original error.
goseg/broadcast/state.go:71-76 shows AddSystemTransitionError can fail (ErrBroadcastRuntimeRequired), so this path can silently lose the failure to persist transition diagnostics.
findings.suggestion: Join and return both errors (primary tick error + transition-log write error) or at minimum emit a guaranteed warning when AddSystemTransitionError fails, so callers and logs see deterministic behavior.

findings.summary: Transition publish collapses context cancellation and timeout into a single sentinel, hiding true cancellation cause.
evidence: goseg/docker/events/events.go:147-149 returns ErrTransitionPublishTimeout for any `<-ctx.Done()` case, regardless of whether the context was canceled or deadline-exceeded.

Callers like goseg/importer/importer.go:606-616 route publish errors through policy handlers, but cannot distinguish explicit cancellation from enqueue timeout because underlying cause is not preserved.
findings.suggestion: Preserve `ctx.Err()` as wrapped cause (for example `fmt.Errorf("transition publish canceled: %w", ctx.Err())`) and keep bus-full as a distinct sentinel; update callers to branch on `errors.Is(..., context.Canceled/context.DeadlineExceeded)` where needed.

findings.summary: Background routine contracts are inconsistent: some services propagate worker failures while backup/chop wrappers cannot report them.
evidence: goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go:574-575 registers routinesystem.RunChopRoutinesWithContext and RunBackupRoutinesWithContext as supervised services expected to return meaningful errors.
goseg/routines/system/system.go:40-49 and 102-109 spawn internal goroutines and always return nil on context exit, so failures inside pass functions cannot surface through supervision.
By contrast, goseg/routines/version.go:95-124 and goseg/routines/logstream/logs.go:91-104,195-205 implement explicit consecutive-failure escalation and return terminal errors.
findings.suggestion: Unify background service error contract: either have backup/chop loops run in the foreground and return terminal errors like other workers, or return an async handle/error channel that startup supervision consumes consistently.

findings.summary: Subsystems depend on mutable process-wide default runtimes instead of explicit wiring
evidence: Each subsystem exposes mutable defaults (`SetDefault...`/`Reset...`) and resolves behavior from those globals during normal operation.
Runtime behavior can change implicitly based on prior package calls/tests, creating non-local dependency and boot-order fragility.
findings.suggestion: Introduce explicit runtime dependency injection at subsystem entrypoints (startup/orchestrator layer), keep global defaults only as compatibility adapters, and migrate call paths to pass runtime handles explicitly.

findings.summary: Similar operational flows apply inconsistent partial-failure handling contracts
evidence: `processConfigEvent` logs persistence failure and still mutates in-memory state, while main config update path returns an error for persistence failures.
`pruneBackups` suppresses file deletion failures and returns nil, but accesspoint cleanup returns immediately on first command error.
findings.suggestion: Define subsystem-level error policy for maintenance/update operations (strict-fail vs best-effort with aggregated errors), implement it consistently, and return structured partial-failure details when continuing after errors.

findings.summary: Multiple wrapper layers add indirection without enough policy/value translation
evidence: Many exported functions are one-step delegates with little transformation, while call chains still route through mutable global runtime state.
Facade layers in auth/click/contextapi increase navigation depth and maintenance surface without clearly reducing coupling.
findings.suggestion: Collapse purely pass-through wrappers, keep only boundaries that enforce policy/translation, and standardize one seam per subsystem (interface at boundary + concrete internal implementation).

findings.summary: Config subsystem mixes unrelated concerns into a high-churn god package
evidence: The same package owns process runtime context setup, file persistence, in-memory container state tracking, networking probes, random/hash utilities, and event handling.
This broad ownership aligns with existing structural signals (flat directory and cohesion warnings) and increases change blast radius.
findings.suggestion: Split config into focused subpackages (runtime context, persisted settings store, volatile runtime state/cache, event handlers) and expose a narrow orchestrated API from `config` as compatibility facade.
